data.title: Gold's safe haven role is shifting as flows override fundamentals, Ben Carlson says
data.description:
Gold’s behavior during recent geopolitical turmoil is challenging its traditional role as a reliable safe haven, according to Ben Carlson on the Investing Experts Podcast.

Historically, gold has tended to move inversely to equities across cycles, often benefiting during periods of market stress. But that relationship has broken down in recent years, with both gold and stocks rising simultaneously, an unusual dynamic that complicates its positioning in portfolios.

Carlson noted that gold’s performance during the latest conflict surprised many investors expecting a classic flight to safety. Instead of rallying further, prices softened.

“A lot of times flows might matter more than fundamentals,” he said, pointing out that assets with the strongest prior returns are often the first to be sold when investors need liquidity.

That dynamic effectively turned gold into a source of cash rather than a refuge. Strong gains leading into the conflict made it one of the easiest assets to sell, reinforcing the idea that positioning and investor behavior can outweigh macro narratives in the short term.

For retail investors, the takeaway is nuanced. Gold still plays a role as a diversifier, but its safe haven status is no longer straightforward. Market structure, liquidity needs, and prior performance trends increasingly shape outcomes, meaning expectations based solely on historical behavior may no longer hold.

data.title: 4 stocks to watch on Thursday: HON, LULU, NVO, BX
data.description:
Stock index futures fell Thursday, a day after Wall Street edged higher with both the Nasdaq and S&P 500 touching record closes.

Here are the four stocks to watch on the day:

Honeywell International (HON) fell 7% in premarket trading after the diversified industrial conglomerate reported mixed quarterly results. The company beat Wall Street earnings expectations but missed on revenue, while a reduced operating cash flow outlook appeared to weigh on shares. As of Wednesday’s close, the stock was up 11% over the prior 12 months.

Lululemon (LULU) dropped 5.84% in premarket trading Wednesday after the athletic apparel retailer announced it appointed longtime Nike executive Heidi O’Neill as its new chief executive officer. O’Neill will start as CEO and join the board effective September 8, 2026, and will be based in Vancouver. The shakeup comes at a crucial time for the athleisure pioneer, as it has been struggling to find its footing due to increased competition from newer rivals such as Vuori and Alo Yoga.

Novo Nordisk (NVO) slipped 1.05% on Thursday after announcing plans for a U.S. launch of the Ozempic pill, an oral version of its popular weight loss drug semaglutide. The announcement followed the GLP-1 drug reaching a main goal in a late-stage trial for children and adolescents with type 2 diabetes, with the treatment outperforming placebo and leading to a 0.83% superior reduction in blood sugar.

Blackstone (BX) reported first-quarter earnings that benefited from continued inflows and market appreciation across most of its strategies. Infrastructure and Tactical Opportunities turned in the strongest returns of the quarter, while its liquid credit and opportunistic real estate strategies had negative returns. Total assets under management reached $1.304 trillion on Dec. 31, 2025, topping the Visible Alpha consensus of $1.277 trillion and rising from $1.275 trillion at the end of Q4 2025.

data.title: Pot stocks rise as Trump reschedules marijuana as a less-dangerous drug: AP
data.description:
Shares of cannabis companies rose in the premarket on Thursday after the Associated Press reported President Donald Trump’s acting attorney general signed an order rescheduling state-licensed medical marijuana as a less-dangerous drug.

Major gainers include Canadian Licensed Producers such as Canopy Growth (CGC), Tilray (TLRY), Cronos (CRON), Aurora Cannabis (ACB), SNDL (SNDL), and OrganiGram Holdings (OGI).

Stocks to watch: U.S. MSOs including Curaleaf Holdings (CURLF), Cresco Labs (CRLBF), Green Thumb (GTBIF), Trulieve Cannabis (TCNNF), Ayr Wellness (AYRWF), TerrAscend (TSNDF), Verano Holdings (VRNO), and Village Farms International (VFF)

Cannabis-focused ETFs: AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF (YOLO), ETFMG Alternative Harvest ETF (MJ), Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (CNBS), AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS)

The order signed by Todd Blanche does not legalize marijuana for medical or recreational use under U.S. law. But it does change the way it's regulated, shifting licensed medical marijuana from Schedule I — reserved for drugs without medical use and with high potential for abuse — to the less strictly regulated Schedule III. It also gives licensed medical marijuana operators a major tax break and eases some barriers to researching cannabis.

Trump told his administration in December to work as quickly as possible to reclassify marijuana. On Saturday, as the Republican president signed an unrelated executive order about psychedelics, he seemed to express frustration that it was taking so long.

Blanche said Thursday that the Department of Justice was “delivering on President Trump’s promise” to expand Americans’ access to medical treatment options. “This rescheduling action allows for research on the safety and efficacy of this substance, ultimately providing patients with better care and doctors with more reliable information,” he said in a statement.

Blanche's action Iargely legitimizes medical marijuana programs in the 40 states that have adopted them. It sets up an expedited system for state-licensed medical marijuana producers and distributors to register with the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration.

It makes clear that cannabis researchers won't be penalized for obtaining state-licensed marijuana or marijuana-derived products for use in their work, and it grants state-licensed medical marijuana companies a windfall by allowing them, for the first time, to deduct business expenses on their federal taxes.

Any marijuana-derived medicine approved by the Food and Drug Administration is similarly listed in Schedule III, it said.

The order represents a major policy shift for the U.S. government, which has continued its longstanding marijuana prohibition — dating to the Marijuana Tax Act of 1937 — even as nearly all the states have approved cannabis use in some form. Two dozen states plus Washington, D.C., have authorized adult recreational use of marijuana, 40 have medical marijuana systems, and eight others allow low-THC cannabis or CBD oil for medical use. Only Idaho and Kansas ban marijuana outright.

The regulation of medical marijuana has come a long way since California became the first state to adopt it in 1996, Blanche wrote.

“Today the vast majority of States maintain comprehensive licensing frameworks governing cultivation, processing, distribution, and dispensing of marijuana for medical purposes,” Blanche wrote. “Taken as a whole, they demonstrate a sustained capacity to achieve the public-interest objectives ... including protecting public health and safety and preventing the diversion of controlled substances into illicit channels.”

Marijuana or marijuana-derived products that are not distributed through a state medical marijuana program will continue to be classified in Schedule I. The Trump administration is launching a new administrative hearing process beginning in June to consider the broader rescheduling of marijuana.

Schedule III drugs are defined as having moderate to low potential for physical and psychological dependence. Some critics of the industry have suggested that legalization in the states has led to stronger and stronger cannabis products, which need to be researched rather than categorized less strictly than before.

The Justice Department under President Joe Biden, a Democrat, had proposed to reclassify marijuana, eliciting nearly 43,000 formal public comments. The Drug Enforcement Administration was still in the review process when Trump succeeded Biden in January, and Trump ordered that process to move along as quickly as legally possible.

Blanche's order sidestepped the review process by relying on a provision of federal law that allows the attorney general to determine the appropriate classification for drugs that the U.S. must regulate pursuant to an international treaty.

It was unclear how the order might affect operations in states where licensed recreational marijuana shops also sell to medical patients. In Washington state, which in 2012 became one of the first states to legalize the adult use of marijuana, 302 of 460 licensed stores have endorsements allowing them to sell tax-free cannabis products to registered patients.

Many Republicans oppose loosening marijuana restrictions. More than 20 Republican senators, several of them staunch Trump allies, signed a letter last year urging the president to keep the current standards.

Trump has made his crusade against other drugs, especially fentanyl, a feature of his second term, ordering U.S. military attacks on Venezuelan and other boats the administration insists are ferrying drugs. He signed another executive order declaring fentanyl a weapon of mass destruction.

The AP contributed to this report.

data.title: Freeport McMoRan falls as Grasberg delay cuts 2026 output outlook
data.description:
Freeport McMoRan (FCX), one of the world’s largest publicly traded copper producers, reported results on Thursday that beat Wall Street expectations for revenue and adjusted earnings, but shares fell sharply after the company reduced 2026 sales forecasts because of delays at its key Grasberg mine in Indonesia.

Freeport McMoRan (FCX) stock fell 8.4% in premarket trading. As of Wednesday’s close, the stock was up 107% over the prior 12 months.

Revenue rose to $6.23 billion from $5.73 billion a year earlier, topping analysts’ consensus estimate of $5.96 billion.

Adjusted net income attributable to common stock was $830 million, or $0.57 a share, above estimates of $0.47 a share.

Net income rose to $881 million, or $0.61 a share, from $352 million, or $0.24 a share, a year earlier.

Why shares are dropping

Despite the earnings beat, investors appeared focused on weaker production guidance tied to operational bottlenecks at Grasberg Block Cave, one of the company’s most valuable assets.

Freeport McMoRan (FCX) cut its 2026 copper sales forecast to 3.1 billion pounds from a January estimate of 3.4 billion pounds. Gold sales guidance was reduced to 650,000 ounces (24,569.59 kg) from 800,000 ounces (30,239.49 kg).

The company said the ramp-up of Grasberg Block Cave has been slowed by wet ore conditions and required modifications to ore loading infrastructure. Near-term production from key blocks is expected to run at about 60% of capacity until upgrades are completed.

Production rates once expected to reach 85% of capacity in the second half of 2026 are now forecast at about 65%, with full capacity pushed closer to late 2027.

CEO stresses long-term strength

“Our first quarter financial results reflect the strength of our diversified portfolio with growth in revenues, cash flow and earnings, compared with last year’s first quarter, despite reduced capacity at our Indonesia operations,” Chief Executive Kathleen Quirk said in the earnings release.

Quirk added that the company remains strongly positioned as “America’s Copper Champion” with geographically diverse operations and a pipeline of growth projects.

Cash flow strong, expansion plans advance

Operating cash flow rose to $1.5 billion from $1.06 billion a year earlier, while capital expenditures totaled $973 million. Freeport McMoRan (FCX) ended the quarter with $3.7 billion in cash and net debt of $2.4 billion, excluding project debt tied to Indonesian downstream facilities.

The company said it continues advancing organic growth plans, including a possible major expansion at El Abra in Chile and brownfield growth projects in Arizona using new leaching technologies.

Freeport McMoRan (FCX) operates major copper, gold and molybdenum mines including the Grasberg minerals district in Indonesia, Morenci in Arizona, Cerro Verde in Peru, Bagdad in Arizona and El Abra in Chile. The company is a major supplier of copper used in power grids, electric vehicles, data centers and industrial infrastructure.

data.title: High Roller Technologies signs marketing agreement with Leverage Game Media
data.description:
• High Roller Technologies (ROLR) has executed a definitive strategic marketing partnership agreement with Leverage Game Media to support customer acquisition and brand awareness initiatives tied to High Roller’s planned launch of a regulated event-based prediction markets product in the United States, the company said on Thursday.
  • Under the terms of the agreement, Leverage Game Media will leverage its digital content platforms, data-driven marketing capabilities, and social media audience to support High Roller’s prediction markets growth strategy. 
  • ROLR +0.68% premarket to $8.5583.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Chicago Fed National Activity Index slumps in March
data.description:
March Chicago Fed National Activity Index: -0.20 vs. +0.03 in February (revised from -11), the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago said on Thursday.

CFNAI three-month average: -0.03 vs. +0.03 prior (revised from -0.01).
